metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a scope bullet explicitly naming a new (or existing) test that injects a non-lock error into `_with_index_lock_retry` and asserts `attempt_count == 1`, so AC3 has a concrete step that reaches it.
findings.finding: AC3 ('Each test asserts a non-lock failure is never retried') requires a test that exercises the non-lock error path, but the scope only mentions the three existing contended-lock tests — no step in the plan creates or modifies a non-lock-failure test case.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to ## Scope: 'Add one parametrized test case (can live in an existing file) that patches `_with_index_lock_retry` to receive a non-lock git error and asserts the seam counter records exactly one attempt.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add to the What/Scope sections a concrete description of how non-lock failures will be injected and verified to not trigger retry (e.g., a patched subprocess that raises a non-lock git error, asserting attempt count stays at 1).
findings.finding: AC3 ('Each test asserts a non-lock failure is never retried') describes a negative-path assertion but neither the What nor the Scope sections describe any work to enable or implement this assertion — there is no described seam, mock, or test pattern for injecting a non-lock error and verifying it does not trigger retry.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria, AC3 vs What / Scope sections

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify in Scope which file/function receives the non-lock-failure assertion (AC#3) — either a new test function in one of the two lifecycle files, or an explicit acknowledgement that `test_event_append_index_lock.py:test_nonlock_failure_is_unaffected` already satisfies it.
findings.finding: AC#3 ('Each test asserts a non-lock failure is never retried') is not currently present in any of the three named contended-lock test functions, and the plan does not specify which file or function should receive this new assertion — making the edit target for this AC line ambiguous/unlocated.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria: '- [ ] Each test asserts a non-lock failure is never retried.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the specific seam mechanism (e.g. 'inject a `_attempt_counter` callback into `_with_index_lock_retry` defaulting to `None`; tests supply a `lambda: counter.increment()`') so the executor has no design choice to make.
findings.finding: The plan defers the seam design to the executor by offering 'barrier or instrumented run_once seam' without specifying which mechanism to implement, what its interface looks like, or how it exposes the attempt count to tests.
findings.suggested_fix: In ## What / ## Scope, replace 'barrier or instrumented run_once seam' with a concrete decision: e.g., 'Add an optional `on_attempt: Callable[[int], None] = None` parameter to `_with_index_lock_retry`; tests pass a counter; default is None (no-op) so production code is unchanged.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a concrete, machine-checkable assertion for AC1, e.g., 'the seam callback receives `attempt_number == 1` with `error_type == index_lock` before the lock file is removed, verified by `assert on_first_attempt_error.called` in the test body.'
findings.finding: AC1 ('First attempt deterministically returns the real/simulated index.lock error before the lock is released') is framed as an implementation property with no proving command — there is no described assertion or command that produces evidence this is true at verification time.
findings.suggested_fix: Rewrite AC1 as: 'The test asserts that the seam callback was invoked with attempt==1 and an index.lock error code BEFORE the lock-removal side-effect fires (verified by assertion order in the test body and confirmed by `make test`).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the two-file vs. three-test contradiction: either name the third test file explicitly in the Why/Scope sections or change 'three' to 'two' throughout.
findings.finding: The 'Why' section names exactly two test files (test_compact_index_lock.py and test_delete_init_index_lock.py), but the 'Scope' section says 'The three contended-lock tests above,' implying a third test exists. No third file is named anywhere in the plan, creating a cross-section contradiction about the actual scope of changes.
findings.location: Why section (file citations) vs Scope section ('The three contended-lock tests above')
findings.suggested_fix: If a third test exists (e.g., test_init_index_lock.py or similar), add it by name to the Why section with its relevant line range. If only two test files are in scope, change Scope to 'The two contended-lock tests above.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the seam interface for the retry-loop instrumentation (e.g., an optional `on_attempt` callback parameter on `_with_index_lock_retry`, a documented test-only slot, or a clear injection point) so the cross-component coupling is explicit, bounded, and removable.
findings.finding: The plan specifies 'add/instrument a seam in the retry loop for tests' but does not define the seam's interface boundary (e.g., callback parameter, module-level hook, dependency injection, or monkey-patchable slot). Without this, the implementation risks silently coupling test infrastructure into production `gitutil.py` code without a clear, documented interface — making the seam hard to discover, maintain, or remove later.
findings.location: Scope — 'add/instrument a seam in the retry loop for tests'; gitutil.py:110-129
findings.suggested_fix: Add a keyword-only parameter `_attempt_hook: Callable[[int, Exception], None] | None = None` to `_with_index_lock_retry`; call it in the loop on each failed attempt. Tests inject a counting hook; production callers pass nothing. Document the parameter as test-only in the function's docstring.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify exactly how the seam is isolated to test execution only (e.g., a `_retry_hook=None` default parameter or a module-level `None`-defaulted slot patched in fixtures), and acknowledge any production callers of `_with_index_lock_retry` that must not be affected.
findings.finding: The plan introduces a seam or barrier into `gitutil.py:_with_index_lock_retry` (production code) without specifying how it is isolated to test-only execution. If the seam is always active (e.g., a module-level callback slot), production callers silently acquire changed behavior — a behavior change the plan does not acknowledge.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a `_retry_hook: Optional[Callable] = None` parameter to `_with_index_lock_retry` (or a module-level `_RETRY_HOOK = None` that tests monkeypatch). Document in the plan that all production call sites pass no hook (None) and that fixtures reset it in teardown.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the retirement/cleanup path for both the synchronization barrier and the `index.lock` file: specify a timeout on the barrier (so tests fail fast rather than hang) and confirm that a `finally`/fixture teardown unlinks the lock file and resets the seam counter regardless of test outcome.
findings.finding: The plan introduces a barrier/counter as shared mutable state inside or alongside `_with_index_lock_retry` for test synchronization, but does not define a RETIRE/cleanup path: if the barrier blocks indefinitely (e.g., the timer thread never fires because the test hangs), the `index.lock` file is never unlinked and leaks into subsequent tests, and the barrier itself is never reset.
findings.location: Scope section / AC 1 ('confirmed first failure using a barrier or instrumented run_once seam')
findings.suggested_fix: Add a `threading.Event` with a `.wait(timeout=2)` guard and a `try/finally` in each test that calls `lock_path.unlink(missing_ok=True)` and resets the seam hook to `None`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the line-number reference in the 'Why' section: the two contended-lock tests in `test_delete_init_index_lock.py` start at lines 98 and 110, not 83 (line 83 is the `_plant_fresh_lock` helper).
findings.finding: The plan cites `test_delete_init_index_lock.py:83-119` as the location of the contended-lock tests, but line 83 is the `_plant_fresh_lock` helper function; the two contended tests begin at lines 98 and 110. An implementor using these line numbers to locate the edit targets will land on the wrong code.
findings.suggested_fix: Change `test_delete_init_index_lock.py:83-119` to `test_delete_init_index_lock.py:98-119` (or name the two functions explicitly).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Enumerate `src/rebar/_store/event_append.py` (two call sites of `_with_index_lock_retry`) in Scope so the implementor confirms the seam design remains backward-compatible with existing callers.
findings.finding: `src/rebar/_store/event_append.py` directly imports and calls `_with_index_lock_retry` (two call sites: lines 211 and 219) and is an unenumerated consumer. If the plan's seam instrumentation changes `_with_index_lock_retry`'s signature (e.g., adds an optional hook parameter), `event_append.py` must remain compatible and is not listed in Scope.
findings.location: Scope section: 'add/instrument a seam in the retry loop for tests'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Narrow the synchronisation mechanism to the instrumented `run_once` seam exclusively (remove 'barrier' as a co-equal option): wrap `run_once` so the test body records each call's result and delays lock removal until at least one lock-error `CompletedProcess` has been observed, making the first-failure guarantee deterministic without relying on thread scheduling.
findings.finding: The plan offers 'a barrier or instrumented run_once seam' as co-equal synchronisation options, but only the seam option is deterministically race-free. A threading.Barrier synchronises Python threads; it does NOT synchronise the moment the external git subprocess observes the index.lock file versus when the timer fires the unlink — the subprocess is spawned via subprocess.run after the Python thread unblocks, leaving the same timing window the plan is trying to close. An implementer choosing the barrier sub-option will still produce a flaky test that can pass through the happy path on slow runners.
findings.location: ## What — 'Synchronize each test to a confirmed first failure using a barrier or instrumented run_once seam'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'a barrier or instrumented run_once seam' with: 'Instrument the `run_once` seam directly: wrap the callable passed to `_with_index_lock_retry` (or monkeypatch it in tests) so the wrapper (a) records each invocation's `CompletedProcess`, and (b) releases the lock only after at least one index.lock-error result is confirmed — an event flag (`threading.Event`) set inside the wrapper and waited on by the timer thread achieves this without relying on OS scheduling.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a positive rationale sentence in the plan (e.g. under ## What or ## Scope) explaining that `_with_index_lock_retry` already exposes the `run_once` callable seam, so instrumentation is purely test-local and requires no production-code changes.
findings.finding: The plan gives no positive rationale for why the seam-based approach was chosen. The existing `run_once: Callable` parameter of `_with_index_lock_retry` is a direct, non-invasive injection point that requires zero production-code changes and keeps the instrumentation test-local — but the plan does not state this. Without the rationale, an implementer might instead reach for heavier alternatives (patching subprocess.run, or restructuring the retry loop to expose an internal counter) that increase blast-radius unnecessarily.
findings.location: ## What / ## Scope — no rationale section present

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
